title: Add Adjacent Angle Measures
description: To find a larger angle composed of two adjacent angles, add their degree measures.
condition: Two adjacent angles share a vertex and form a larger angle; their individual measures are given.
response: Add the two angle measures to get the total measure of the larger angle.
label: Principle
example: If one angle is $38^\circ$ and the adjacent angle is $11^\circ$, then the larger angle is $38^\circ + 11^\circ = 49^\circ$.
notes: Used to find $\angle CPA$. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Classify Acute Angle
description: Identify an angle as acute when its measure is less than 90 degrees.
condition: An angle formed by two rays measures less than 90°.
response: State that the angle is an acute angle.
label: Concept
example: An angle measuring $45^\circ$ is an acute angle.

title: Classify Angle by Measure
description: Determine whether an angle is acute, right, or obtuse based on its measure.
condition: An angle with a known or estimated measure.
response: State whether the angle is acute (<90°), right (=90°), or obtuse (>90° and <180°).
label: Concept
example: An angle measuring $120^\circ$ is obtuse because it is greater than $90^\circ$ and less than $180^\circ$.
notes: [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Classify Obtuse Angle
description: Identify an angle as obtuse when its measure is greater than 90 degrees and less than 180 degrees.
condition: An angle formed by two rays measures greater than 90° and less than 180°.
response: State that the angle is an obtuse angle.
label: Concept
example: An angle measuring $120^\circ$ is an obtuse angle.

title: Classify Right Angle
description: Identify an angle as a right angle when it measures exactly 90 degrees.
condition: An angle formed by two perpendicular rays measures 90°.
response: State that the angle is a right angle.
label: Concept
example: An angle measuring $90^\circ$ is a right angle.

title: Classify Triangle by Angle Type
description: Classify a triangle as acute, right, or obtuse based on the measures of its interior angles.
condition: Given a triangle with its angle measures known or visible (acute < 90°, right = 90°, obtuse > 90°).
response: State whether the triangle is acute (all angles < 90°), right (one angle = 90°), or obtuse (one angle > 90°).
label: Concept
example: If a triangle has angles $90^\circ$, $45^\circ$, and $45^\circ$, it is a right triangle because one angle is $90^\circ$.
notes: Requires knowledge of angle definitions.

title: Classify Triangle by Side Lengths
description: Classify a triangle as equilateral, isosceles, or scalene based on the number of equal side lengths.
condition: Given a triangle with side lengths marked or stated.
response: State whether the triangle is equilateral (3 equal sides), isosceles (2 equal sides), or scalene (no equal sides).
label: Concept
example: If a triangle has two sides marked congruent, then it is an isosceles triangle.
notes: Relies on conventional definitions of triangle types.

title: Complement within 90 Degrees
description: Find a missing angle within a right angle by subtracting known adjacent angles from 90°.
condition: Angles that together form a right angle (90°) with one or more known measures.
response: Subtract known angle measures from $90^\circ$ to find the unknown angle.
label: Principle
example: If $\angle DPA = 90^\circ$ and one part is $51^\circ$, then the other part is $90^\circ - 51^\circ = 39^\circ$.

title: Compute Difference
description: Subtract one integer degree measure from another to find the result.
condition: Expression of the form a − b with known integers.
response: Compute the numerical difference.
label: Production / Skill
notes: Arithmetic computation step.

title: Convert Clock Minutes to Degrees
description: Determine the degree rotation of a clock’s minute hand based on elapsed minutes.
condition: A time interval in minutes for the minute hand.
response: Multiply minutes by 6° (since 360° ÷ 60 minutes = 6° per minute).
label: Principle / Model
example: $30 \text{ minutes} \times 6^\circ = 180^\circ$
notes: Based on a full circle (360°) divided into 60 equal minute intervals.

title: Convert Fraction of Circle to Degrees
description: Convert a fractional part of a circle into degrees using 360° as the whole.
condition: A fraction of a circle (e.g., 1/8) is given.
response: Multiply the fraction by 360° to find the degree measure.
label: Principle
example: $\frac{1}{8} \times 360^\circ = 45^\circ$.
notes: Uses the fact that a full circle is 360°. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies]

title: Count Pairs of Parallel Sides
description: Determine how many distinct pairs of parallel sides a polygon has.
condition: Given a polygon with sides visible.
response: Identify and count each pair of sides that are parallel.
label: Production / Skill
example: In a rectangle, the top and bottom sides form one pair of parallel sides, and the left and right sides form a second pair, for a total of 2 pairs.
notes: Student visually inspects orientation of sides.

title: Determine Direction After Clockwise Rotation
description: Identify the new facing direction after rotating a given number of degrees clockwise.
condition: A starting direction and a clockwise rotation measure.
response: State the new direction or person faced.
label: Production / Skill
example: Starting facing Gabe, a $90^\circ$ clockwise turn results in facing Robin.
notes: Spatial reasoning around a circle.

title: Draw Angle of Given Measure
description: Construct an angle of a specified degree measure using a protractor.
condition: A given base ray and a target angle measure (e.g., 112°, 95°).
response: Mark the target degree on the protractor and draw a second ray through that mark; add an arc to indicate the angle.
label: Production / Skill
example: To draw $112^\circ$, mark $112^\circ$ from the baseline and draw a ray through the mark.
notes: Includes marking the angle with an arc as part of the construction.

title: Equilateral Triangle Angle Measure
description: Each interior angle of an equilateral triangle measures 60°.
condition: A triangle is identified as equilateral.
response: State that each interior angle measures 60°.
label: Fact
example: In an equilateral triangle, each angle is $60°$.
notes: Relies on definition of equilateral triangle.

title: Express Angle as Fraction of Circle
description: Write an angle measure as a fraction of 360°.
condition: An angle measure in degrees.
response: Write the angle over 360 as a fraction.
label: Principle
example: A $47^\circ$ angle is $\frac{47}{360}$ of a circle.

title: Find Missing Angle in Linear Pair
description: When two adjacent angles form a straight line (180°), subtract the known angle from 180° to find the missing angle.
condition: Two adjacent angles form a straight angle (sum 180°) and one angle measure is given.
response: Compute missing angle as 180° − (given angle or sum of given adjacent angles).
label: Principle
example: If one angle is 47°, the adjacent linear-pair angle is $180 - 47 = 133°$.
notes: Includes cases where multiple adjacent angles are summed before subtracting.

title: Identify a Line of Symmetry
description: Determine whether a drawn line divides a figure into two mirror-image halves.
condition: A figure with a proposed line is shown.
response: State whether the line is a line of symmetry (yes/no).
label: Category
example: A vertical line through the center of an isosceles triangle divides it into two congruent mirror halves, so it is a line of symmetry.
notes: Relies on visual/spatial recognition. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Identify a Right Triangle
description: Determine whether a triangle contains a right (90°) angle.
condition: A triangle is shown with or without angle markings.
response: Classify it as a right triangle or not a right triangle.
label: Category
example: A triangle with one $90^\circ$ angle marking is a right triangle.
notes: Based on presence of a 90° angle.

title: Identify Angle Figure
description: Recognize a figure formed by two rays with a common endpoint as an angle.
condition: A figure shows two rays sharing a common endpoint.
response: Name the figure as an angle.
label: Concept
example: Rays $\overrightarrow{ED}$ and $\overrightarrow{EF}$ share endpoint $E$, so they form an angle.

title: Identify Line Figure
description: Recognize a straight path extending infinitely in both directions as a line.
condition: A straight figure has arrowheads on both ends indicating infinite extension.
response: Name the figure as a line.
label: Concept
example: A figure with arrows on both ends through points $I$ and $J$ is a line $\overleftrightarrow{IJ}$.

title: Identify Line Segment Figure
description: Recognize a straight figure with two endpoints and no arrows as a line segment.
condition: A straight figure has two endpoints marked and no arrows.
response: Name the figure as a line segment.
label: Concept
example: The figure connecting points $A$ and $B$ with endpoints is line segment $\overline{AB}$.

title: Identify Neither Parallel Nor Perpendicular
description: Determine that two lines are neither parallel nor perpendicular when they intersect at a non-right angle or do not meet the criteria for either.
condition: Two lines either intersect at a non-90° angle or have different directions and would meet if extended.
response: State that the lines are neither parallel nor perpendicular.
label: Concept
example: If two lines intersect forming a $60^\circ$ angle, they are neither parallel nor perpendicular.

title: Identify Obtuse Angle in Triangle
description: Determine whether a triangle contains an obtuse angle (greater than 90°).
condition: Given a triangle with its angles visible or measurable.
response: State whether the triangle has at least one obtuse angle.
label: Concept
example: If one angle in a triangle measures $110^\circ$, then the triangle has an obtuse angle.
notes: Uses definition of obtuse angle (> 90°).

title: Identify Parallel Lines
description: Recognize when two line segments are parallel (same direction, never intersect).
condition: Two labeled line segments are shown in a figure.
response: State that the segments are parallel.
label: Category
example: If segments $\overline{HO}$ and $\overline{KL}$ run horizontally and never meet, then $\overline{HO} \parallel \overline{KL}$.
notes: Based on visual direction and non-intersection. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Identify Perpendicular Lines
description: Recognize when two line segments intersect to form a right angle.
condition: Two labeled line segments intersect in a figure.
response: State that the segments are perpendicular.
label: Category
example: If $\overline{NM}$ meets $\overline{ML}$ at a $90^\circ$ angle, then $\overline{NM} \perp \overline{ML}$.
notes: Often indicated by a square corner mark. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Identify Point Figure
description: Recognize a single labeled dot as a point.
condition: A figure shows a single dot labeled with a capital letter.
response: Name the figure as a point.
label: Concept
example: A single dot labeled $C$ represents point $C$.

title: Identify Ray Figure
description: Recognize a figure with one endpoint and one arrow as a ray.
condition: A straight figure has one endpoint and one arrow indicating infinite extension in one direction.
response: Name the figure as a ray.
label: Concept
example: A figure starting at point $G$ and extending through $H$ with one arrow is ray $\overrightarrow{GH}$.

title: Identify Vertical and Horizontal Symmetry Lines
description: Determine all vertical and horizontal lines that create mirror symmetry in a figure.
condition: A symmetric figure.
response: Draw or state all valid symmetry lines.
label: Production / Skill
example: A rectangle-like figure with matching corners has one vertical and one horizontal line of symmetry through its center.
notes: Non-verbal judgment of matching halves.

title: Match Polygon to Group by Parallel Sides
description: Determine whether a polygon belongs to a group defined by a specific number of parallel side pairs.
condition: Given a polygon and a rule such as 'exactly one pair of parallel sides' or 'more than one pair of parallel sides.'
response: Assign the polygon to the group whose rule matches its number of parallel side pairs.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If a polygon has exactly one pair of parallel sides, then it belongs in the group defined as 'exactly one pair of parallel sides.'
notes: Builds on counting parallel side pairs.

title: Measure Angle Using Protractor
description: Determine the degree measure of an angle by aligning and reading a protractor.
condition: A drawn angle and a correctly aligned protractor.
response: Read and state the degree marking where the second ray intersects the scale.
label: Production / Skill
example: The second ray intersects the scale at $114^\circ$, so the angle measures $114^\circ$.
notes: Procedural measurement skill.

title: Select Correct Protractor Scale
description: Choose the correct inner or outer scale on a protractor based on the orientation of the initial ray.
condition: An angle aligned on a protractor with one ray on the baseline.
response: Read the scale that begins at 0° on the side of the initial ray.
label: Category
example: If the initial ray points right, read the scale starting at $0^\circ$ on the right.
notes: Often implicit in protractor use instruction.

title: Write Equation for Missing Angle
description: Translate a geometric relationship into a subtraction equation to represent a missing angle.
condition: A full angle measure (e.g., 60° or 180°) and a known part are given; need unknown part.
response: Write equation of form variable = total − known part.
label: Principle
example: If one part of a 60° angle is 38°, write $t = 60 - 38$.
